title: The Small-Chunk, Big-Context Problem
concept: Small chunks retrieve precisely, but the model needs surrounding context to answer well — motivating a two-tier approach.

exercises.prompt: Before we dive into hierarchical relationships, what is the main goal of text chunking in a basic RAG system?

options.text: Breaking documents into smaller text segments for retrieval.
Encrypting database keys to prevent unauthorized access.
Translating text from one language to another automatically.

exercises.explain: Welcome to Unit 432! Let's start with a foundational concept: chunking is the practice of breaking down large documents into smaller text snippets so a retriever can find them.

cards.title: The Retrieval Paradox
cards.body: When building LLM apps, we face a paradox: small chunks make search highly accurate, but they starve the LLM of the bigger picture it needs to write a good response.

cards.title: The Dilemma Defined
cards.body: The Small-Chunk, Big-Context Dilemma is the trade-off in RAG systems: small text chunks are easy for search engines to find, but LLMs need the broader context around those chunks to generate accurate answers.

cards.title: The Snipped Textbook
cards.body: Imagine search-indexing a textbook by cutting it into individual sentences. While you can find the exact sentence containing 'caffeine' instantly, you cannot answer how it affects the heart without reading the surrounding paragraphs.

cards.title: The Mystery 'It'
cards.body: Searching for 'operating temperature' might find a chunk saying 'It is 72°F.' But without the parent context, the LLM doesn't know if 'It' refers to the battery, the engine, or the room.

cards.title: Is Smaller Always Better?
cards.body: People think using tiny chunks is always better because it reduces noise. But tiny chunks strip away the narrative flow and pronouns, leaving the LLM confused about the topic.

cards.title: Precision vs. Context
cards.body: Compare how different chunk sizes behave during search retrieval versus LLM generation.

left.text: Easy to match search queries, but lacks broader story.

right.text: Harder to match precisely, but contains the full story.

exercises.prompt: Why does using exclusively tiny chunks create a problem for LLM applications?

options.text: They are too large for the LLM's prompt window.
They match search queries easily but lack the surrounding information the LLM needs.
They confuse the vector database because they contain too much narrative detail.
They require too many complex mathematical calculations to index.

exercises.explain: Small chunks have very specific vector representations, making them easy to match against search queries, but they lack the surrounding details needed for synthesis.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a small chunk perfectly matches a search query, it is guaranteed to contain all the context the LLM needs to write a response.
exercises.explain: Small chunks are great for matching the user's search query, but they lack the narrative context needed for the LLM to generate a complete answer.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing piece that LLMs require when processing highly specific search results:
exercises.sentence: A search engine can easily find a small chunk, but the LLM needs the surrounding context to generate a meaningful answer.
exercises.explain: Without surrounding context, the retrieved text snippets lack the background information the LLM needs to understand the full picture.

exercises.prompt: Which of these text chunks is an AI-generated example of a 'loss of context' chunk that makes it impossible to know what 'It' refers to?

options.text: The standard operating temperature for the Model X solar inverter is 45 degrees Celsius.
It should not exceed 45 degrees Celsius under normal operating conditions.
Ensure the solar inverter is kept in a ventilated space to maintain its 45 degrees Celsius limit.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by an AI attempting to write a tiny, context-less chunk that lacks the noun reference ('It') required to understand what is being discussed.

exercises.prompt: If small chunks are great for finding specific facts but terrible for answering complex questions due to missing context, how does a two-tier (parent-child) chunking system solve this?

options.text: We should only retrieve and feed massive 10,000-word documents to the LLM.
Search for small, specific sentences but pass their larger parent paragraphs to the LLM.
Avoid using chunks entirely and pass the entire database as a single prompt.
Use small chunks for both retrieval and the final LLM response generation.

exercises.explain: To get the best of both worlds, we search using small, precise chunks (high retrieval accuracy) but feed the corresponding larger parent chunks (rich context) to the LLM to write the response.
